quick_summary: Angular is a TypeScript-based frontend framework for building single-page applications using a component architecture with built-in dependency injection, routing, and reactive forms. It matters for interviews because enterprises (banks, healthcare, government) heavily adopt Angular for its opinionated structure and TypeScript-first approach.

detailed_content.explanation:
Angular applications are built from components — self-contained units with a TypeScript class, an HTML template, and optional styles. Think of components as custom HTML elements: you create `<app-header>` or `<user-card>` and Angular renders them with their own logic and appearance.

The template binding system is the heart of Angular. Data flows from the component class to the template via interpolation `{{ value }}` and property binding `[src]="imageUrl"`. Events flow back via event binding `(click)="handleClick()"`. Two-way binding `[(ngModel)]="name"` combines both. This unidirectional data flow with explicit event handling makes state changes predictable and debuggable.
detailed_content.key_takeaway: Angular's component model enforces unidirectional data flow — @Input for parent-to-child, @Output with EventEmitter for child-to-parent — making state changes traceable and testable.
detailed_content.try_it: Create a parent component that holds a list of products. Build a child ProductCardComponent that receives a product via @Input and emits an addToCart event via @Output. Display the cart count in the parent.
detailed_content.complexity: Time: O(1) per component render, Space: O(n) for n tracked components in change detection

pro_tip: In Angular interviews, always mention OnPush change detection strategy — it's the single biggest performance optimization most teams overlook. Explain that the default strategy checks every component on every event, while OnPush only checks when @Input references change or events fire from that component.
